Dual-frequency SAR images exhibit the complementary properties in the resolution and scattering characteristics, thus fusing dual-frequency SAR images can combine these advantages to provide the richer and more comprehensive information for downstream semantic segmentation tasks. However, existing fusion algorithms typically assume preregistered images and focus on the good visual effects while neglecting the semantic requirements of high-level vision tasks. To address this problem, a joint registration and fusion network for the dual-frequency SAR images based on the semantic information is proposed, which can achieve the high-precision cross-frequency registration and high-quality image fusion. First, in the image registration stage, the microwave vision data semantic pseudocolor maps are introduced as the auxiliary information, and the semantic-consistency loss is used to guide the registration process, finally the fusion loss is fed back to the image registration as an optimization signal. Second, in the image fusion stage, the global spatial attention mechanism is used for the feature extraction and fusion, and then the semantic segmentation loss is incorporated to guide the image fusion so that the results can better conform to the high-level semantic structures. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness and correctness of the proposed method, which can outperform state-of-the-art approaches in the image registration accuracy, image fusion quality, and downstream semantic segmentation performance.
author: Mengfan Ge, Mingjun Huang, Hongtu Xie, Bin Xu, Haozong Liu, Yuanjie Zhang, Kaiwen Qiu, Nannan Zhu

title: TFFENet: Temporal Difference Fusion and Frequency-Domain Feature Ehancement Network for Remote Sensing Image Change Detection
content: Remote sensing change detection (RSCD) is an important technique for monitoring geographical change. However, under the influence of illumination factors such as imaging angle difference and illumination conditions, it is still a difficult task to accurately identify the change region. In addition, the existing methods only use limited information from bitemporal images, which leads to the loss of details such as small targets and change boundaries, thus leading to false detection. To address these limitations, we propose a novel network termed temporal difference fusion and frequency-domain feature enhancement network (TFFENet). First, we extract multilevel hierarchical features from the Siamese hierarchical backbone. Subsequently, a temporal difference module is designed to generate differential features to enhance contour information. Moreover, a global feature enhancement module is introduced to strengthen the modeling of global contextual information. To effectively extract and refine texture features while mitigating the impact of illumination variations, we employ a frequency feature refinement module that fuses spatial and frequency information for adaptive feature filtering. Finally, to fully integrate heterogeneous feature representations, a depth feature extraction module is proposed to aggregate interlayer contextual information and capture complementary feature semantics. To verify the effectiveness of the proposed TFFENet, extensive experiments were carried out on three public RSCD benchmark datasets, namely LEVIR-CD, WHU-CD, and SYSU-CD. The results show that TFFENet has achieved the best F1 values on all datasets, which are 91.91%, 94.99%, and 82.63%, respectively. The results consistently show superior performance compared to existing state-of-the-art methods.
author: Junwei Li, Peng Yu, Rihai Lai, Shuaiao Li, Xuefeng Ma

title: Dual-Level Domain Alignment and Boundary-Aware Optimization for Semisupervised Tree Crown Instance Segmentation in UAV Imagery
content: This paper addresses the key task of accurately delineating individual tree crowns from uncrewed aerial vehicle (UAV) imagery for fine-scale orchard monitoring. A core challenge lies in the significant performance degradation when models are transferred across orchards with differing illumination, canopy structures, and background conditions. Semisupervised domain adaptation (SSDA) offers a label-efficient solution. However, existing methods predominantly align global features while neglecting instance-level discrepancies critical in dense canopy scenes, and conventional loss functions lack effective boundary supervision. To overcome these limitations, we propose the Dual-Focus and Alignment Network (DFANet). This framework integrates semisupervised learning with dual-level domain alignment and boundary-aware mask optimization. Specifically, a Mean Teacher strategy is first employed to leverage unlabeled target images via consistency regularization. Second, a dual-level adversarial network aligns feature distributions at both the multi-scale feature level and the instance level to reduce domain divergence in dense canopy environments. Third, a dual-focus Dice loss is proposed to enhance mask accuracy by jointly optimizing the interior regions and boundary bands, thereby improving crown separation in adhesion-prone areas. Experiments conducted on three UAV datasets under six bidirectional transfer settings demonstrate that, with only 10% of labeled target data, DFANet achieves superior cross-domain robustness compared to all supervised, semisupervised, unsupervised domain adaptation, and SSDA counterparts. Ablation studies confirm the complementary roles of dual-level alignment and boundary-focused optimization. Feature space visualizations further illustrate their contribution to improved crossdomain consistency. The results indicate that DFANet provides an effective and labelefficient solution for deploying crown segmentation models across heterogeneous orchard environments.
author: Shaohang Wu, Boyuan Zhuang, Zhipan Wang, Qingling Zhang

title: LLCEFA: A Plug-and-Play Lightweight Local Contrast and Edge Fusion Approach for Infrared Small Target Detection Enhancement
content: The use of local contrast and edge information as prior knowledge is common in hybrid infrared small target detection methods. However, existing methods tightly integrate prior knowledge modules with the backbone network, limiting their flexibility. To address this issue, we propose a lightweight local contrast and edge fusion approach (LLCEFA), featuring a dual-branch architecture with a main branch and an edge branch designed to fully leverage the synergistic advantages of prior knowledge guidance and data-driven learning. Within the main branch, the proposed local contrast attention fusion (LCAF) module leverages the local contrast prior to reconstruct the input image into more discriminative feature representations. Concurrently, the edge branch employs the developed adaptive edge weight extraction (AEWE) module to independently derive edge weights for enhancing intermediate-layer features. This design effectively decouples prior knowledge processing from the backbone's architecture, allowing LLCEFA to serve as a plug-and-play enhancer compatible with various backbone methods, achieving a performance boost with introducing only a minimal additional burden. Experimental results validate our LLCEFA's efficacy in enhancing backbone networks, achieving average performance gains of 2.233%,1.826%, and 1.056% in intersection over union ($IoU$), normalized Intersection over Union ($nIoU$), and probability of detection (${P_{d}}$), respectively, while false-alarm rate (${F_{a}}$) is reduced by an average of 6.729 × 10${^{-6}}$ across three public datasets. This comes at an average increase of only 0.0009 M parameters and 0.0617G floating-point operations added to the backbone, highlighting its high efficiency and broad applicability.
author: Peichao Wang, Renke Kou, Jiabao Wang, Rui Zhang

title: DMHA-Transformer With Joint Convolution and Global Attention Enhancement for Hyperspectral Image and LiDAR Classification
content: Hyperspectral image (HSI) categorization plays a pivotal role in various Earth observation applications. Single-modal HSI is constrained by insufficient spatial resolution, spectral redundancy, and noise contamination, making it inadequate for high-accuracy land-cover classification and complex scene interpretation; thus, complementary multisource information is urgently needed. Existing convolution neural network (CNN)-based approaches fail to adequately exploit the global sequential nature of spectral features, while vision transformers and related architectures incorporate a randomly initialized external CLS tokens, whose inductive bias often results in limited generalization. Given these limitations, we, therefore, develop JGAM-DMHA-Net, a new network architecture dedicated to joint classification by HSI and light detection and ranging (LiDAR) data. The framework employs an enhanced dual attention for efficaciously combining supplementary information from HSI with LiDAR data. A joint network incorporating hybrid 3D-CNN, 2D-CNN, and global attention mechanism preserves both channel-wise and spatial-wise information by simultaneously modeling the interactions across channel, height, and width dimensions. It dynamically reweights the importance of different locations or channels on a global scale, enabling more effective information exchange and representation learning. In the DMHA-Transformer block, depth-wise convolution not only excavates deeper-level features but also establishes global dependencies among features at disparate spatial locations by leveraging complementary information sources, thereby endowing the model with superior generalization capability. The experimental results on three public datasets indicate that the employed approach outperforms several state-of-the-art methods, with improvements of 0.95%, 0.09%, and 2.89%, respectively. The results provide clear evidence validating the advantages of our model in achieving efficient multisource data fusion, offering a practical solution for complex scenarios.
author: Chunyan Chen, Guosheng Han, Chengjun Wang

title: Geological Remote Sensing Interpretation via Multilevel Feature Integration Network
content: Geological remote sensing interpretation enables the extraction of task-specific geological information from imagery, playing a vital role in geological investigation and analysis. Nevertheless, deep learning-based methods still lag behind expert visual interpretation in terms of accuracy, owing to the high spectral similarity, fragmented spatial distributions, and class imbalance of geological elements. This study proposes a multilevel feature integration network (MLFIN) to address the above challenges in multiclass geological element interpretation. Specifically, a class-wise random sampling strategy is designed to alleviate class distribution imbalance, improving the capability of MLFIN to learn features from diverse geological elements. The encode–decode modules can enhance feature representation and improve the utilization of element information. The former integrates convolution operations with attention mechanisms to extract multiscale spatial–spectral features, while the latter enables the adaptive fusion of multilevel features. To mitigate the boundary ambiguity segmentation, the global attention module models long-range contextual dependencies, while the segmentation module refines spatial features, enriching the spatial details of geological elements and strengthening boundary feature discrimination. Experimental results on two geological datasets indicate that MLFIN outperforms current deep learning-based segmentation methods, achieving Overall Accuracy value of 72.83% and 67.36%, and mean Intersection-over-Union values of 55.6% and 46.16%, respectively. It validates the strong generalization capacity across diverse geological environments and proves the effectiveness of its submodule in multiclass geological interpretation tasks.
author: Ying Cao, Qing Cheng, Zhijun Zhang

title: CMTDet: Complementary Mask-Enhanced Transformer for Multimodal Remote Sensing Object Detection
content: Object detection based on multimodal images is an emerging method in remote sensing which has gained extensive attention in recent years, particularly for all-weather robust detection applications. However, in multimodal remote sensing object detection, there are issues of overreliance on a single modality and intermodal interference. To solve these problems, this article proposes a complementary mask-enhanced transformer detection network (CMTDet). Two key modules are designed in CMTDet: the dual-stage cross-modal interaction (DCMI) module and the common-mode guided weighted fusion (CMGF) module. The DCMI module consists of two components: the differential guided complementary enhancement (DGCE) submodule and the complementary mask cross-attention (CMCA) mechanism. The DGCE component captures differential information from features of different modalities, enhancing the ability to express the semantic-channel correlation and differential information between modalities. Then, the CMCA component explores the correspondence between features of different modalities through cross-attention and reduces the dependence of feature extraction on a single modality using asymmetric complementary masks. After the improved feature extraction, the CMGF module generates guidance signals based on common-mode information, explores common information between modalities and the contribution of information across different modalities, and designs adaptive weights to retain the advantageous information of each modality. Ultimately, this enhances the semantic expression ability and robustness of the fused modality. Extensive experiments on the DroneVehicle, VEDAI and OGSOD datasets show that CMTDet outperforms the baseline: it achieves 1.87% and 1.73% higher mAP50 and mAP50-95 on the DroneVehicle dataset, with a 1.98% mAP boost in nighttime scenarios, verifying its superior detection performance.
author: Jianfeng Li, Siyu Cheng, Xiang Li, Longqing Tu, Yingjie Mei, Guangjiao Zhou, Chenxu Wang

title: Dual-Teacher Cross-Modal Ship Detection With Edge-Constrained SAR-Style Generation
content: Ship detection is an important research direction in the intelligent interpretation of remote sensing imagery, with significant applications in marine monitoring, maritime traffic management, and search and rescue. Synthetic aperture radar (SAR) imagery plays a crucial role in maritime scenarios due to its all-weather, day-and-night imaging capabilities, making it a vital data source for ship detection. However, single-modality data are often constrained by imaging conditions and insufficient for detection in complex environments. Optical and SAR images each offer unique advantages, and their complementary nature motivates cross-modal fusion to enhance detection performance. To alleviate the reliance on limited annotated SAR data, this article proposes an edge-constrained CycleGAN to generate structurally consistent SAR-style pseudoimages from unpaired optical images, thereby enhancing cross-modal feature alignment and increasing data diversity. To further address the complexity of learning unified representations across modalities within the teacher–student framework, a dual teacher–student architecture based on the probabilistic ensembling fusion strategy is designed to extract domain-invariant features from both optical and SAR pseudosamples and to optimize the student network through collaborative distillation. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves better overall detection performance compared to representative semisupervised methods in multisource remote sensing ship detection, validating its effectiveness for SAR ship detection under complex remote sensing conditions.
author: Juanping Wu, Chenxing Mao, Weiwei Guo, Zenghui Zhang

title: Target Decomposition Feature-Guided Multimodal Domain Generalization Learning for Unsupervised PolSAR Image Classification
content: Cross-domain (CD) models have been successfully applied to polarimetric synthetic aperture radar (PolSAR) image classification to alleviate the scarcity of labeled samples. However, these models often struggle to extract strongly generalizable domain-invariant features when encountering significant domain shifts. Furthermore, prevailing methods predominantly operate within the supervised domain adaptation paradigm, requiring access to target-domain data during training, which inherently limits their generalizability to entirely unseen target domains. Therefore, this article proposes a target decomposition feature-guided multimodal domain generalization learning network (MMDGN), inspired by multimodality and domain generalization, for unsupervised classification of target-domain PolSAR images under the same frequency constraint. The core idea is to leverage the physical scattering mechanism consistency of the same frequency and categories to guide CD feature optimization, thereby enhancing the discriminative power and domain invariance of the features. The proposed MMDGN comprises three key modules: a multimodal feature extraction module that separately extracts image-modal and text-modal features, the latter derived from category-specific representative scattering components; a pattern memory-based space adjustment module that projects both modalities into a unified embedding space; and a dual-level semantic alignment module that integrates supervised contrastive learning with adversarial training to align multimodal features at both intraclass and interclass levels. This promotes that the optimized image-modal features, guided by the text modality, exhibit intraclass compactness and interclass separability. Experiments on six CD tasks constructed from three $C$-band PolSAR datasets demonstrate that MMDGN achieves state-of-the-art performance in unsupervised classification accuracy and CD generalization.
author: Yice Cao, Wenxin Yao, Zhenhua Wu, Jie Chen, Lei Zhang, Zhixiang Huang

title: Nationwide Spatial Distribution of Floating Photovoltaic Power Stations in China From High-Resolution Remote Sensing
content: Floating photovoltaics (FPVs) are gaining significant attention within the global shift to clean energy due to their efficient use of water surfaces and environmental benefits. However, research and accurate spatial data for FPV significantly lag behind land-based PV systems, particularly for large-scale deployments. Challenges including complex water environments, irregular panel layouts, and monitoring limitations have hindered the development of effective extraction methods, preventing accurate assessment of FPV distribution and spatial patterns needed for informed planning. To address this gap, this study proposes an intelligent FPV identification method based on an improved deep learning model, Solar-BiSeNetV2, applied to high-resolution remote sensing imagery for accurate extraction across complex water environments. We subsequently analyzed the national spatial pattern of FPV and its influencing factors. Results show that the proposed Solar-BiSeNetV2 model achieved high accuracy and efficiency, improving intersection over union by 6.2% and reducing the missed detection rate by 21.4% compared to baseline models, while maintaining a real-time inference speed of 47.3 FPS. The total area of FPV in China reached 412 km2. The spatial analysis revealed a distinct pattern of FPV distribution: dense in the east, sparse in the west, and concentrated near water bodies. This study provides the first high-precision national FPV distribution map for China and introduces an efficient, robust methodology for large-scale FPV extraction. The resulting data and spatial analysis offer valuable insights and a quantitative basis for scientific planning, potential assessment, and sustainable development strategies for FPV projects.
author: Maoteng Zheng, Gan Zhang, Yue Yu, Yanran Xiang, Luotao Liu

title: A Decoder-Enhanced Network With Multiscale Aggregation for Multiclass Remote Sensing Change Detection
content: Multiclass land-cover change detection from bitemporal remote sensing imagery has become a critical task for large-scale Earth observation. Nevertheless, prevailing segmentation-based change detectors remain prone to false alarms and missed detections, especially along fine-grained boundaries and in complex backgrounds where unchanged regions are easily confused with particular change classes. To mitigate these issues, we propose a decoder-enhanced UNet-style segmentation framework (USNet) to improve change-class discrimination by jointly reinforcing semantic representation, boundary/detail reconstruction, and computational efficiency. Specifically, USNet strengthens the bottleneck to enrich high-level contextual semantics while adopting lightweight convolutional blocks to constrain decoding complexity. Moreover, spatial-and-channel squeeze excitation recalibration is incorporated into the upsampling stages to suppress background interference and facilitate accurate boundary recovery. Extensive experiments on the SECOND dataset demonstrate that USNet consistently outperforms representative segmentation baselines. In particular, it achieves 72.69% mean intersection over union, 49.35% F1-score, and 88.97% accuracy, surpassing strong competitors, such as FCN, UNet, PSPNet, and DeepLabV3+, across all evaluation metrics. These results confirm the effectiveness of USNet for robust multiclass land-cover change detection in challenging remote sensing scenarios.
author: Lijia Chen, Zhenrong Zhuang, Mingyan Ke, Jian Chen

title: Hierarchical Joint Optimization Algorithm With Joint Weight Guidance for ALB Multistrip Registration
content: In recent years, airborne LiDAR bathymetry (ALB) has become increasingly prevalent for surveying nearshore shallow waters. However, due to system errors and marine environment complexities, depth deviations often arise between adjacent strips. While these can be mitigated through registration, errors introduced during this process may propagate and accumulate across multiple strips. In ALB multistrip registration, both error accumulation and computational cost present significant, underexplored challenges. To address these issues, a hierarchical joint optimization algorithm with a joint weight guidance mechanism is introduced, specifically tailored to reference-free multistrip registration. The algorithm refines the graph model voting mechanism by leveraging the inherent characteristics of the ALB point cloud and, through a clustering-based approach, enables adaptive selection of inliers to facilitate coarse registration between adjacent strips. Recognizing the impact of coarse registration accuracy and the quality of correspondences on fine registration, a joint weighting scheme is devised to guide subsequent optimization. Finally, a hierarchical joint optimization strategy is proposed to mitigate cumulative errors and reduce computational complexity, where the strip with the highest accuracy in the coarse registration stage is fixed as the reference strip to prevent error drift. The experiment uses two sets of real-world data from the South China Sea, each with differing terrain complexities, and the results exhibit a high degree of consistency with manually registered data. This study offers a detailed discussion of the key components of the proposed algorithm, comparing it with the designed benchmark method, and highlights the algorithm’s significant advantages in reducing cumulative errors and enhancing computational efficiency.
author: Dongdong Pu, Hongzhou Chai, Chao Dong, Yongzhong Ouyang

title: Multitask Joint Optimization Based SAR Target Recognition Model From Echo Data
content: To address the limitations of conventional synthetic aperture radar (SAR) target recognition, particularly the computational redundancy and error accumulation inherent in the imaging-first paradigm, the paper proposes echo-to-class network (E2CNet), an end-to-end recognition model operating directly on echo data via multitask joint optimization. By bypassing intermediate imaging procedures, the proposed E2CNet directly maps echo data to target categories. Central to the E2CNet architecture is a deep complex-valued neural network designed to preserve and leverage scattering characteristics within the echo data. A bidirectional long short-term memory (Bi-LSTM) network is subsequently integrated to capture spatiotemporal dependencies in the feature sequences. Furthermore, a scattering point adaptive extraction strategy, guided by SAR image priors, is incorporated into a multitask learning framework to enhance both recognition accuracy and scattering center localization precision. Experiments on multicategory echo datasets derived from the MSTAR and FUSAR-Ship benchmarks demonstrate that the E2CNet achieves competitive recognition performance, attaining accuracies of 86.45% and 91.60%, respectively. Moreover, validations on real-world data from the Fucheng-1 satellite further confirm the recognition capability of the model and its advantage in reducing end-to-end inference time. The results highlight the potential of E2CNet for efficient and accurate SAR target recognition in practical applications.
author: Yiwen Wang, Fuxuan Cai, Jingjing Zhang, Zhongyuan Ji, Hui Bi

title: An Efficient Approach Integrating Sentinel-1 and Sentinel-2 Imagery for Mapping Permanent and Seasonal Water Area
content: Accurate mapping of permanent and seasonal water (SW) is crucial for understanding water resource dynamics and anthropogenic impacts. However, in mountainous and cloud-prone environments, single-source remote sensing data often fail to balance spatiotemporal continuity and mapping accuracy. To address temporal mismatches between optical and synthetic aperture radar (SAR) imagery and exploit their distinct noise characteristics, this article proposed an efficient surface water area (SWA) mapping framework based on integrating Sentinel-1 (S1) and Sentinel-2 (S2) imagery, enabling efficient extraction of permanent and SW over large and complex landscapes. The Central Yunnan Plateau was selected as the study area, and mapping accuracy was validated using in situ uncrewed aerial vehicle (UAV) imagery. Under identical parameter settings, SWA was independently derived using S1 and S2 imagery to quantitatively assess the performance of different data sources across diverse environments. Results indicate that the proposed integration method consistently outperforms single-source approaches and identifies approximately 5% more SWA than the JRC Yearly Water Classification History v1.4 (JRC YWCH v1.4). Furthermore, the distinct noise sources associated with optical and SAR imagery facilitate complementary error suppression within the integrated framework, enhancing the robustness of permanent and SW classification.
author: Yang Zhang, Qi Yi, Zhengbao Sun, Tingping Wei

title: Decoupling Static Context and Dynamic Change: A Spatiotemporal Decoder With Time-Averaged Priors for Multidecadal Heritage Monitoring
content: The long-term preservation of Urban Cultural Heritage (UCH), a key directive of the United Nations’ Sustainable Development Goal 11.4, requires robust monitoring of multidecadal landscape dynamics driven by urbanization. However, analyzing the necessary long-term satellite archives reveals a significant spatiotemporal challenge. This multidecade data presents two interwoven problems: first, complex, nonstationary temporal dynamics that require processing the entire sequence history to distinguish evolving characteristics; and second, a severe spatial ambiguity in recent imagery, where heritage sites become spectrally indistinguishable from the surrounding, dynamically evolved urban sprawl. This dual challenge renders conventional methods, which are often purely spatial or purely temporal, ineffective at resolving this ambiguity. To address this, we first introduce a novel, multidecadal (1984–2024) spatiotemporal dataset for Xi’an, China, specifically curated to study this problem. We then propose a novel architectural framework designed to solve this conundrum by decoupling static spatial context from dynamic temporal evolution. Our primary contribution is a new decoding strategy that directly confronts the spatial ambiguity. Instead of sourcing skip connections from a single, ambiguous, recent frame, our decoder is fed a time-averaged composite derived from the entire sequence. This composite acts as a robust spatial prior that inherently enhances the separability between stable heritage sites and their evolved urban surroundings. Evaluated on our dataset, the proposed framework achieves state-of-the-art performance (leading by 2.6% –10.7% in mIoU) and yields a 3.2% mIoU gain attributable to the static-prior decoder in ablation studies, contributing significant improvement independent of the specific temporal model used.
author: Lixian Zhang, Binxiao Liu, Pengming Feng, Tianxiang Hao, Huiyue Tang, Zijian Ouyang, Ying Yang, Shaoheng Tan, Siyu Yue

title: MTU-Net: A Multiperspective Transformer U-Net With Water Index Channel-Spatial Attentions Embedding for Small Water Body Extraction From PlanetScope Imagery
content: Deep learning has been widely used in mapping small water bodies (SWBs) with very high-resolution PlanetScope imagery. Traditional convolutional neural network and transformer hybrid networks, which rely on single-scale patches for feature embedding at each stage, often introduce artifacts at feature boundaries and compromise their structural integrity. Moreover, current models do not adequately account for the shape complexity of SWBs, which is a critical factor in mapping performance. To address these limitations, this article proposed a hybrid multiperspective transformer-based U-Net (MTU-Net). MTU-Net synergistically learns global context by extending single scale to multiscale embedding with multiple path transformer. In the feature fusion stage, MTU-Net fuses shallow and deep features through spatial and channel attention and considers the water index to preserve rich spatial details and semantic information simultaneously. Furthermore, MTU-Net employs a shape-area adaptive-weighted binary cross-entropy loss function, which dynamically adjusts sample weights during training by simultaneously considering the shape complexity and size of water bodies. Quantitative experiments demonstrate that MTU-Net achieves the highest accuracies F1 of 94.11% and an Intersection over Union of 88.87% compared with the state-of-the-art networks.
author: Pu Zhou, Yuyang Li, Yalan Wang, Xiang Li, Runsheng Ma, Yihang Zhang, Sisi Li, Yun Du, Xiaodong Li

title: Mapping Woody Vegetation From Multisource Imagery and Prediction Fusion for Enhanced Data Efficiency and Accuracy
content: Tree cover maps are a fundamental remote sensing product, used to derive ecological insights about the landscape and are essential to change detection, vegetation mapping, and fire monitoring programs. However, comprehensive tree cover mapping requires reliable and high-quality imagery, free of cloud and weather defects to ensure accurate model outputs. Deep learning approaches can generate high quality maps with minimal human intervention but require large amounts of human annotated data to be successful. In this work we propose a framework consisting of methods that aim to improve the data efficiency and robustness of deep learning models using data fusion techniques to segment woody vegetation defined as vegetation over the height of 2 m across the state of New South Wales, Australia. To improve robustness against varying image quality, we propose an image composition method that normalizes the imagery and removes defects, whilst also minimizing the reliance on individual image quality by proposing a prediction fusion method. The two methods resulted in an error reduction of 38.2% and 53.6%, respectively compared to single-source imagery. To address deep learning approaches' limitation of requiring large amounts of data, we apply label transfer to multiple sources of imagery as a form of data augmentation to improve data efficiency. Learning from multiple image sources was shown to be the biggest improvement in performance, resulting in an error reduction between 28.1% and 76.2% across the different validation experiments, while reducing the standard deviation of performance across image dates by a factor of 13.
author: Kal Backman, Jared Wood, Adam Roff

title: FOREST-GC: A conFOrmable Rendering Engine for Synthetic Tree Generation and Counting
content: Accurate tree counting from satellite imagery remains a critical challenge for agricultural monitoring, climate analysis, and land-use management. While recent advances in deep learning, particularly large pretrained vision-language models (VLMs), have achieved remarkable results in general visual tasks, we show that they fail to reliably address tree counting in high-resolution satellite imagery. This highlights the need for specialized datasets to support the development and evaluation of models capable of solving this task, especially in plantation environments. To this end, we introduce a configurable synthetic image generator that produces realistic satellite imagery of olive plantations along with precise annotations in terms of the number and positions of trees, providing valuable resources for advancing tree counting research in agricultural settings. The generator simulates terrain textures, spatial distributions of trees, and tree shadows to approximate real-world conditions and exposes over 100 customizable parameters to control appearance and layout. It also includes a simulated near-infrared layer to support vegetation-specific spectral analysis. We compare deep learning methods based on object detection or segmentation models trained on the generated data with pretrained VLMs and an unsupervised computer vision baseline, and show that synthetic training achieves superior accuracy on a manually annotated real-world dataset, demonstrating effective sim-to-real performance transfer specifically in olive plantation imagery. In particular, SAM-ViT-large achieves an average relative error of 4.82% on the real data, which is comparable to the performance of human labelers. The generator source code is publicly available, supporting further research in data-scarce agricultural monitoring with a focus on plantation analysis.
author: Luciano Prono, Najmeddine Dhieb, Philippe Bich, Chiara Boretti, Fabio Pareschi, Marco Brini, Hakim Ghazzai, Riccardo Rovatti, Gianluca Setti

title: Robust Visual–Inertial Odometry With Point-Line Feature Fusion for Enhanced Localization in Weak-Textured Environments
content: The visual–inertial odometry (VIO) system, integrating a camera and an inertial measurement unit, is a fundamental technology for autonomous localization in mobile devices. However, conventional VIO algorithms that rely solely on point feature extraction suffer from poor robustness in weak-textured environments. To address this limitation, this article proposes an enhanced VIO algorithm that fuses point-line features. First, line features are introduced, and the edge drawing lines segment detection algorithm is improved through a hierarchical multiscale collaborative detection mechanism and a cross-scale geometric consistency fusion strategy. These enhancements effectively mitigate feature fragmentation caused by noise and broken line segments. Second, an adaptive weighting approach is used to adjust the influence of point-line features in real time during the optimization process, ensuring effective and reliable feature utilization. In addition, a keyframe-based sliding-window optimization framework is implemented to balance computational efficiency while enhancing pose estimation accuracy. Experimental evaluations on the EuRoC and KITTI dataset demonstrate that the proposed algorithm significantly improves localization performance in weak-textured scenarios, this results in improved robustness and accuracy for the VIO system as a whole.
author: Jin Sun, Xinwen Zhu, Haitao Zhao

title: Assessing Long-Term Spatiotemporal Dynamics of Microphytobenthos in a Korean Intertidal Flat Using Harmonized Landsat and Sentinel-2 (HLS) Data
content: Microphytobenthos (MPB) are key benthic microalgae that support intertidal ecosystem functioning by driving primary production, stabilizing surface sediments, and contributing to carbon storage. Because these ecological roles are closely tied to the biomass and distribution of MPB, understanding their spatiotemporal patterns is essential for evaluating the functioning and resilience of tidal-flat ecosystems. Satellite-based approaches have demonstrated their value for characterizing seasonal variability and elevation-dependent differences in MPB biomass across a range of estuarine and coastal environments worldwide. Korean tidal flats, shaped by large tidal ranges and spatially variable Holocene sediments, present steep elevation–exposure gradients that are expected to influence MPB distribution. Yet how these gradients translate into seasonal and interannual biomass patterns has not been examined using multiyear satellite observations. We address this gap by proposing and implementing a satellite-based monitoring framework that couples ay and a waterline-derived elevation model. The algorithm developed for the Geunso Bay tidal flat ac site-specific chlorophyll-a concentration (Chl-a) retrieval algorithm with Harmonized Landsat and Sentinel-2 imagerhieved an R2 of 0.70 and a root mean squared error of 18.33 mg/m2 in estimating Chl-a of MPB. The framework produced multiyear seasonal maps and resolved elevation-dependent variability, exhibiting summer maxima in the upper flat, winter maxima in the middle flat, and mixed responses in the lower flat. This study delivers the first multiyear satellite record of MPB in a Korean tidal flat and demonstrates a transferable, cost-effective approach for benthic monitoring.
author: Sooyoon Koh, Seungil Baek, Jong Hyuk Lee, Jaehoon Noh, Howon Lee, Myung Jin Hyun, Wonkook Kim

title: HAB-Net: Bidirectional CNN-Transformer With Hierarchical Alignment for Remote Sensing Scene Classification
content: The fusion of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and vision transformers (ViTs) is a powerful strategy for remote sensing scene classification, leveraging their complementary strengths in local texture and global context modeling. To fully exploit these complementary strengths for addressing the interclass similarity and high intraclass variability caused by the complex spatial arrangements of RS scenes, this work introduces the hierarchically aligned bidirectional CNN-Transformer network (HAB-Net), which fosters deeper, two-way integration through two novel components. First, hierarchical attention gating (HAG) distills multilevel attention from the ViT into spatial gates, compelling the CNN to focus on semantically critical regions. In turn, CNN-driven feedback attention (CFA) completes the loop by feeding these enhanced, detail-rich CNN features back into the ViT, refining its tokens via cross-attention. Ultimately, a dual-stream head merges these mutually reinforced features for scene classification. Extensive experiments on the UCM, AID, and NWPU-RESISC45 benchmarks demonstrate that HAB-Net achieves state-of-the-art performance. Ablation studies confirm the indispensable contributions of both HAG and CFA, validating the effectiveness of the proposed deep bidirectional integration strategy.
author: Xi Gong, Xuejing Xie, Yongyang Xu, Zhong Xie

title: Chip Industry Week In Review
content:
Arm uncorked its first internally developed CPU chip this week, aimed squarely at the agentic AI data center market. Arm CEO Rene Haas (pictured) emphasized the CPU’s power efficiency and performance/watt compared to other AI processor architectures. “We are obsessed with efficiency, and if you think about one of the biggest appeals that Arm has had over the years, it is power profile,” he said. “If you think about 1 gigawatt of capacity and the CapEx associated with that extra power you’re spending at the sake of performance, it’s up to $10 billion CapEx.” Arm tapped its ecosystem to market and support the new chip, the first version of which is based on a 3nm TSMC process.

Meanwhile, Gartner predicts that by 2030, performing inference on a 1 trillion-parameter LLM will cost GenAI providers 90% less than it did in 2025, driven by gains in semiconductor efficiency, infrastructure, model design, chip utilization, inference-specific silicon, and edge processing.

Google predicts 2029 is the year quantum computers can break today’s encryption, an accelerated timeline that will require migration to quantum-resistant algorithms.

GlobalFoundries filed patent infringement lawsuits against Tower Semiconductor, alleging Tower infringed on 11 GF patents covering manufacturing process technologies used in products for smart mobile, automotive, aerospace, and communications infrastructure markets.

Buildouts

  • Elon Musk formally unveiled its plans for its Texas Terafab, an estimated $20B to $25B chip fab that Tesla, SpaceX, and xAI are jointly building. Musk’s stated goal is “to produce over a terawatt of compute per year (logic, memory & packaging) with ~80% for space and ~20% for the ground.”
  • Advantest will establish a new R&D site in Saitama, Japan, to connect its major development locations, expand lab capacity, and strengthen recruitment in the Greater Tokyo area.
  • Lumentum will establish a 240,000 square-foot manufacturing facility in Greensboro, North Carolina, to produce InP-based optical devices.
  • Apple expanded its American Manufacturing Program with Bosch, Cirrus Logic, TDK, and Qnity Electronics, and said it plans to spend $400M on the new programs through 2030.

Big Deals

  • Lace Lithography raised $40M to advance a helium atom beam lithography approach. The company claims it can achieve a beam width of just 0.1 nanometers compared to the 13.5nm wavelength used by ASML’s EUV scanners, and is targeting a pilot fab test tool around 2029, reports Reuters.
  • Taiwanese memory chip maker Nanya collectively raised ~US$2.5B in private funding from Sandisk, Solidigm, Kioxia, and Cisco.
  • AI chip startup Rebellions will receive ~US$166M in funding, marking the first direct investment under Korea’s “K-Nvidia” initiative.
  • Kandou AI raised $225M to focus on signaling and SerDes technologies for AI systems and high-speed rack connectivity.
  • Normal Computing raised $50M for its AI-powered EDA platform, which it uses to design silicon IP for thermodynamic computing.

JEDEC updated the LPDDR5/5X Serial Presence Detect (SPD) standard, adding support for calculating recovery time when switching operating modes.

Global

Americas

  • The SIA urged global trade negotiators to renew and make permanent the World Trade Organization moratorium on customs duties on electronic transmissions, arguing that the policy has supported more than 25 years of growth in digital trade across sectors including semiconductors, AI, and electronics.
  • Canada intends to spend ~US$655M to boost defense and dual-use technologies, including quantum computing, communications, and sensors.

Europe

  • SEMI Europe welcomed the European Commission’s implementation dialogue on a possible “Chips Act 2.0,” saying the process gives industry a channel to help shape the next phase of Europe’s semiconductor policy and investment framework.
  • Rigetti Computing plans to invest up to $100M in the UK to deploy a quantum computer with over 1,000 qubits in the next several years.

Asia

  • SK Hynix is planning a potential public listing in the U.S. later this year.
  • Air Liquide inaugurated its first large-scale advanced materials manufacturing plant in Taiwan, which will produce deposition and etching materials for nanoscale processes such as ALD used in next-generation AI and HPC chips.
  • Aixtron is building a new Malaysia plant for its deposition equipment for compound semiconductors.

Research

imec and Ghent University researchers incorporated thin-film lithium niobate modulators onto imec’s silicon photonics platform using a micro-transfer printing method.

A new 2D Materials Roadmap covers high-impact 2D materials, including graphene and its derivatives, TMDs, MXenes, as well as their heterostructures and moiré systems.

Argonne National Laboratory researchers developed a chip that compresses and processes pixel-detector data in real-time, allowing engineers to analyze experiments and adjust conditions while they are still running. This breakthrough offers value for the semiconductor research ecosystem, where faster handling of imaging and detector data can accelerate materials analysis, process development, and other data-intensive R&D workflows.

UIUC researchers reported a vertical photoconductive intrinsic diamond switch capable of handling 17.1A at 1kV. The work points to continued progress in diamond-based power electronics, where the material’s high breakdown field and thermal properties have long made it attractive for high-voltage, high-efficiency switching.

Cambridge-led researchers created a new type of hafnium oxide memristor to store and process data in the same place, a design that offers more stable, low-power operation and could cut AI energy use by up to ~70%.

IBM’s quantum computer was used to simulate real magnetic materials with results that match neutron scattering experiments.

Security

Intel’s security group INT31 detailed real-world implementation challenges of post-quantum cryptography — specifically those associated with NIST‘s new lattice-based PQ standards,  FIPS 203 and FIPS 204 — which promise strong mathematical security.

Matthew Ferren at the Council of Foreign Relations argues that the Trump administration’s recently released abbreviated cyber strategy reflects a big gap between the vision of U.S. cyberspace dominance and “the government’s capacity to deliver on it,” leaving Americans more exposed than necessary. Further, the U.S. Cyber Command leader has no direct cybersecurity experience, according to the report.

New releases and deals

  • Intel released its 2026 Platform Security Report, an annual data-driven report on how Intel products performed, emphasizing its confidential AI, post‑quantum readiness, and HW enforced software robustness.
  • OpenAI introduced a safety bug bounty program this week, aiming to partner with security and safety researchers.
  • At the RSA Conference this week, Swissbit presented its hardware platform to test PQC authentication flows.
  • Rowhammer discoverer Onur Mutlu just released a lecture on “Revisiting RowHammer – Top Picks in HW and Embedded Security.”
  • GF customers will now have access to Jmem Tek’s PUF IP for HW root of trust and PQC security technologies.
  • Siemens Smart Infrastructure and Viakoo are teaming up on advanced OT/IoT security management.
  • Yubico will partner with IBM and Auth0 to create a ‘human-in-the-loop’ model for the approval of AI agent workflows.

Government

New Technology

Synopsys is supporting the development of Arm’s new AGI CPU with a full-stack set of tools spanning EDA, interface IP, and hardware-assisted verification. The collaboration covers design, signoff, IP integration, emulation, and prototyping for the CPU, which is built on Arm Neoverse CSS V3.

Siemens launched Teamcenter Digital Reality Viewer and Digital Twin Composer in India, as well as announced its participation in ARPA-H, an initiative aimed to improve indoor air quality.

Infineon announced a new TLVR quad-phase module that exceeds 2 A/mm² for AI compute.

Alibaba announced its latest RISC-V-based CPU core, which can be customized for specific inference patterns and is targeted for cloud computing and AI agents.

Huawei announced an AI inference accelerator card that it says was developed entirely with Chinese technology.

Innatera introduced a community-driven, open repository for edge AI models and full processing pipelines designed for neuromorphic hardware.

Renesas deployed Arteris’ FlexNoC interconnect IP for its R-Car Gen 5 SoC series tailored for ADAS and automated driving.

Automotive

Keysight introduced new in-vehicle network test solutions to address safety and reliability.

A coalition of automotive trade organizations has pushed for the Trump administration to keep auto import restrictions on Chinese vehicles, contending they “pose a direct threat to America’s global competitiveness, national security, and automotive industrial base,” reports Nikkei Asia.

The European Automobile Manufacturers’ Association supports the potential free trade agreement between Australia and the EU, urging timely approval.

Autonomous

  • Amazon-owned Zoox is expanding its robotaxi service to San Francisco, Las Vegas, Austin, and Miami.
  • GM will begin testing autonomous vehicles on supervised public-roads for Chevrolet and Cadillac vehicles.
  • ZYT claims its new autonomous driving tech can outdrive the company’s CEO.
  • BYD’s ‘God’s Eye’ driver-assistance feature, which should be able to identify dangers along the road, has experienced a number of glitches, including jerking into adjacent lanes and almost hitting oncoming traffic, reports Bloomberg.

Electric

  • Tesla may introduce automated and wireless charging for semi trucks, potentially driving over a pad to start charging.
  • E-trucks from China may dominate the European Union market, due to lower prices and EU’s lack of domestic battery manufacturing and charging ecosystem.

The University of Arizona, Taiwan’s National Sun Yat-sen University, and others inked a deal to jointly advance research and workforce development. The partnership will build up the strength of supply chains and increase research partnerships.

title: Integrating Error Propagation Theory Into the FMEDA Framework (Robert Bosch GmbH)
content:
A new technical paper, “Quantifying Uncertainty in FMEDA Safety Metrics: An Error Propagation Approach for Enhanced ASIC Verification,” was published by Robert Bosch GmbH.

Abstract

“Accurate and reliable safety metrics are paramount for functional safety verification of ASICs in automotive systems. Traditional FMEDA (Failure Modes, Effects, and Diagnostic Analysis) metrics, such as SPFM (Single Point Fault Metric) and LFM (Latent Fault Metric), depend on the precision of failure mode distribution (FMD) and diagnostic coverage (DC) estimations. This reliance can often leads to significant, unquantified uncertainties and a dependency on expert judgment, compromising the quality of the safety analysis. This paper proposes a novel approach that introduces error propagation theory into the calculation of FMEDA safety metrics. By quantifying the maximum deviation and providing confidence intervals for SPFM and LFM, our method offers a direct measure of analysis quality. Furthermore, we introduce an Error Importance Identifier (EII) to pinpoint the primary sources of uncertainty, guiding targeted improvements. This approach significantly enhances the transparency and trustworthiness of FMEDA, enabling more robust ASIC safety verification for ISO 26262 compliance, addressing a longstanding open question in the functional safety community.”

Find the technical paper here. March 2026.

Armato, Antonino, Christian Kehl, and Sebastian Fischer. “Quantifying Uncertainty in FMEDA Safety Metrics: An Error Propagation Approach for Enhanced ASIC Verification.” arXiv preprint arXiv:2603.21770 (2026).

The post Integrating Error Propagation Theory Into the FMEDA Framework (Robert Bosch GmbH) appeared first on Semiconductor Engineering.

title: In-Depth Analysis of 187 Publications on Hardware Reverse Engineering (Ruhr U., MPI)
content:
A new technical paper, “SoK: From Silicon to Netlist and Beyond Two Decades of Hardware Reverse Engineering Research,” was published by the Ruhr University Bochum and the Max Planck Institute for Security and Privacy.

Abstract

“As hardware serves as the root of trust in modern computing systems, Hardware Reverse Engineering (HRE) is foundational for security assurance. In practice, HRE enables critical security applications, including design verification, supply-chain assurance, and vulnerability discovery. Over the past two decades, academic research on Integrated Circuit (IC), Field-Programmable Gate Array (FPGA), and netlist reverse engineering has steadily grown. However, knowledge remains fragmented across domains and communities, which complicates assessing the state of the art and hampers identifying shared research challenges. In this paper, we present a systematization of knowledge based on an in-depth analysis of 187 peer-reviewed publications. Using this corpus, we characterize technical methods across the HRE workflow and identify technical and organizational challenges that impede research progress. We analyze all 30 artifacts from our corpus using established artifact evaluation practices. Key results could be reproduced for only seven publications (4%). Based on our findings, we derive stakeholder-centric recommendations for academia, industry, and government to enable more coordinated and reproducible HRE research. These recommendations target three cross-cutting opportunities: (i) improving reproducibility and reuse via artifact-centric practices, (ii) enabling rigorous comparability through standardized benchmarks and evaluation metrics, and (iii) improving legal clarity for public HRE research.”

Find the technical paper here. March 2026.

Karadağ, Zehra, Simon Klix, René Walendy, Felix Hahn, Kolja Dorschel, Julian Speith, Christof Paar, and Steffen Becker. “SoK: From Silicon to Netlist and Beyond $-$ Two Decades of Hardware Reverse Engineering Research.” arXiv preprint arXiv:2603.17883 (2026).

The post In-Depth Analysis of 187 Publications on Hardware Reverse Engineering (Ruhr U., MPI) appeared first on Semiconductor Engineering.

title: Systematic Analysis of CPU-Induced Slowdowns in Multi-GPU LLM Inference (Georgia Tech)
content:
A new technical paper, “Characterizing CPU-Induced Slowdowns in Multi-GPU LLM Inference,” was published by the Georgia Institute of Technology.

Abstract

“Large-scale machine learning workloads increasingly rely on multi-GPU systems, yet their performance is often limited by an overlooked component: the CPU. Through a detailed study of modern large language model (LLM) inference and serving workloads, we find that multi-GPU performance frequently degrades not because GPUs are saturated, but because CPUs fail to keep the GPUs busy. Under limited CPU allocations, systems exhibit symptoms such as delayed kernel launch, stalled communication, and increased tokenization latency, leading to severe GPU underutilization even when ample GPU resources are available. This work presents a systematic analysis of CPU-induced slowdowns in multi-GPU LLM inference. We show that these bottlenecks persist even in serving stacks that employ process-level separation and modern GPU-side optimizations such as CUDA Graphs. Since the marginal cost of additional CPU cores is small relative to GPU instance pricing, our evaluation indicates that increasing the number of CPU cores can substantially improve performance and stability at minimal additional cost. Under moderate serving load, we observe that CPU-starved configurations frequently time out, while providing adequate CPU resources restores responsiveness and reduces time-to-first-token (TTFT) latency by 1.36-5.40x across configurations, all without requiring additional GPUs. This work shows that CPU provisioning is a crucial factor in multi-GPU LLM inference configuration, helping prevent control-side bottlenecks.”

Find the technical paper here. March 2026.

Chung, Euijun, Yuxiao Jia, Aaron Jezghani, and Hyesoon Kim. “Characterizing CPU-Induced Slowdowns in Multi-GPU LLM Inference.” arXiv preprint arXiv:2603.22774 (2026).

The post Systematic Analysis of CPU-Induced Slowdowns in Multi-GPU LLM Inference (Georgia Tech) appeared first on Semiconductor Engineering.
